title: Chapter 9: I Know
content: Chloe didn't sleep.

Two words on a folded piece of paper, delivered by a security guard who'd winked at the camera. Two words that could mean anything. *I know* what you're doing and I'm going to stop you. *I know* what you're doing and I want to help. *I know* what you're doing and I'm testing to see if you'll confess.

The ambiguity was the point. The guard — whose name, Chloe had learned from Sophia, was Vasquez — had given her just enough information to panic without giving her enough to act. It was a move Chloe recognized because she'd used it herself, in her viral threads, in her psychological jiu-jitsu with Holloway. Give someone a piece of information. Watch how they react. The reaction tells you everything.
Vasquez was running the same playbook.
At breakfast, Chloe sat down next to Layla and slid the note across the table.
Layla read it. Her face didn't change — she was getting better at that — but her fingers stopped tapping. "Who?"
"Vasquez. The new night guard."
"The one who looks like she's killed people."
"The one who winked at the camera before she delivered it."

Layla turned the paper over. The handwriting was sharp, angular, the letters carved deep into the paper like the writer had been pressing hard enough to leave grooves. "This isn't a threat," Layla said. "A threat would be more specific. 'I know about the tablets.' 'I know about the email.' This is... an opening."
"An opening for what?"
"For us to make the first move."
Chloe considered this. "She's giving us the initiative. Which means either she's on our side and wants us to trust her, or she's working for Sterling and wants us to incriminate ourselves."
"How do we find out which?"
"We give her something that looks like a confession but isn't."
Layla's eyes sharpened. "A canary trap."
"A canary trap." Chloe leaned back. "We feed her information that's true but harmless. If Sterling acts on it, we know she's his. If she doesn't pass it on, we know she's ours."
The decoy plan went into motion that afternoon.

Sophia, who'd been tracking staff schedules for eight months, confirmed that Kevin from IT took his lunch break from 12:15 to 12:45 — the only thirty-minute window in the entire day when the server room was unattended. The external security consultant Sterling had brought in — a man named Brock, whose LinkedIn Layla had managed to pull before the network went dark — took his lunch at the same time. They ate together. Two men, two sandwiches, thirty minutes of unsupervised server room.
"That's our window," Chloe said. "Layla, can you activate the cafeteria Bluetooth from here?"
"I can enable it remotely. I set up a cron job on the terminal before the network locked down. It activates at 12:20 and broadcasts for exactly ten minutes."
"What does it broadcast?"
"A Bluetooth device name. 'HP-OfficeJet-4500.' It's the most common office printer in North America. Generic enough to not be suspicious, active enough to trigger a spectrum analyzer alert."
Maya snorted. "You're framing a printer."
"I'm framing a printer that doesn't exist," Layla said. "On a terminal that isn't on the network map. In a cafeteria that no one monitors during lunch."
Chloe smiled. "Bestie, that's beautiful."
At 12:20 precisely, the phantom printer began broadcasting.

Chloe was in Group Therapy C — the one she'd promised Holloway she'd attend — when she saw Brock walk past the window. Fast. Purposeful. Not the casual patrol of someone monitoring a facility, but the focused movement of someone who'd just gotten an alert on a device Chloe couldn't see.
He was heading toward the cafeteria.

She kept her face neutral. The group therapist — a woman named Dr. Liu who was the only staff member Chloe hadn't figured out yet, because Dr. Liu seemed genuinely kind and that was either a trap or a miracle — was leading a discussion about "healthy coping mechanisms."
"Chloe," Dr. Liu said, "you've been quiet today. Any thoughts?"
Chloe looked at her. At the woman's unreadable, possibly-genuine, possibly-tactical warmth.
"I think," Chloe said, "that the healthiest coping mechanism is knowing the difference between a problem you can solve and a problem you can outlast."
Dr. Liu's expression flickered — not the Holloway micro-flinch, but something more complex. Curiosity, maybe. Or recognition.
"And which are you working on right now?" she asked.
Chloe smiled — the real one, the one she saved for the laundry room. "Both."

Brock spent forty-five minutes in the cafeteria searching for a printer that didn't exist. The Bluetooth signal stopped broadcasting at 12:30, but he stayed until 1:05, running his spectrum analyzer along every wall, every cabinet, every piece of kitchen equipment.
By the time he left, his face was the face of a man who knew he'd been played but couldn't prove it.
Layla watched it all from the laundry room window.
"He's angry," she reported that night. "Not at us. He doesn't know it was us. He's angry at himself. He thinks he missed something."
"Good," Chloe said. "Angry people make mistakes."
That night, during her shift, Vasquez stopped at Chloe's door again.
This time Chloe was ready. She'd written a response — not a confession, but an opening. The canary trap, baited with truth that couldn't hurt them.
*I'm not what they say I am. None of us are. If you know what that means, leave another note. If you don't, this paper never existed.*
She'd left it under her own door, folded the same way Vasquez had folded hers.

At 2:17 AM — two minutes after Rodriguez's old call time, and Chloe filed that away, because Vasquez knew Rodriguez's schedule and that meant Vasquez had studied this place before she arrived — the paper disappeared.
A hand. A flicker. Gone.
Chloe lay in the dark, pulse hammering, waiting.
Five minutes passed. Ten. The camera blinked its red heartbeat. The lavender-scented air hummed through the vents.
Then the paper slid back under the door.
Chloe unfolded it. Two words again, same sharp handwriting, same carved-deep pressure:
*I'M NOT.*
Below it, a third word. Smaller. Almost an afterthought.
*EITHER.*
Chloe stared at the paper for a long time. Then she smiled in the dark, where the camera couldn't see.
They had a fifth.
